role_summary: Co-founder of the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party with his brother Aaron Dixon (1968), the first chapter formed outside California.
ai_reading: Elmer Dixon's position on the Nonviolence as Theology vs Armed Self-Defense axis is -1.00, the maximal reading in the entire 136-interview corpus toward the Nonviolence pole. He is the catalog's clearest case of an AI-vs-culture inversion: a co-founder of the Seattle Black Panther Party chapter (the first chapter outside California), whose cultural placement is unambiguously the armed-self-defense pole, lands at the absolute opposite end of the axis the corpus defines. His top semantic neighbor is his brother Aaron (entry 1) at 388, then Evans Derrell Hopkins (entry 42), Elbert Howard (entry 35), and Kathleen Cleaver (entry 73), all BPP figures who also cluster at the Nonviolence end. The five-BPP-at-Nonviolence-pole pattern repeats across every member of the cohort in the corpus. Either the BPP testimony tradition's redemptive, community-service-oriented vocabulary (the Free Breakfast for Children program, the Seattle clinic that became Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center) dominates the vector signature over the party's rhetorical surface, or the axis polarity itself is in tension with the political reading, an open question this catalog leaves for the paper-in-progress.
biographical_paragraph: Elmer Dixon helped establish the Seattle chapter of the Black Panther Party in 1968 alongside his brother Aaron Dixon, the chapter being the first formed outside California [src: 1]. In April 1968, the week of Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination, the brothers attended the West Coast Black Student Union conference in San Francisco and were at Bobby Hutton's funeral; in the days surrounding those events they met with Black Panther leadership including Bobby Seale and Kathleen Cleaver, beginning the conversation that produced the Seattle chapter [src: 1]. His brother Aaron is interviewee entry 1 in this archive; their joint founding of the Seattle chapter is also the seed for Aaron's 2012 memoir My People Are Rising [src: 1]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 37 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Elmer Dixon oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
